"And all the congregation lifted up their voice, and cried; and the people wept that night." The witnesses note the whole congregation breaking out in a loud and clamorous weeping at the spies' evil report. The witnesses note that Gill notes the outcry began with the princes and elders with Moses, but quickly spread through the body of the people, very loud and clamorous, with shrieks and tears. The witnesses note that the report of the ten spies had filled them with despair. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all the congregation — the whole camp in outcry; the lifted up their voice — loud and clamorous lamentation; the wept that night — a night of useless tears; the the fruit of unbelief — grieving for fear, not for sin.

The witnesses agree the weeping was the fruit of unbelief: the people, hearing only the giants and the walled cities and not the promise of God, gave themselves to a night of useless tears; their sorrow was not for sin but for fear, lamenting as lost what God had sworn to give — a grief that, refusing to trust him, wept where it ought to have believed.

2:

The witnesses note the people murmuring against their leaders, wishing they had died in Egypt or the wilderness. The witnesses note that Gill notes they murmured against Moses and Aaron as the instruments of bringing them out of Egypt. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the murmured against Moses — blaming their leaders; the would we had died in Egypt — preferring the grave to the land; the or in this wilderness — despising the inheritance offered; the taken at their word — their wish to die there fulfilled.

The witnesses agree the murmuring reached a desperate folly: they wished themselves dead rather than go up to the good land, preferring the graves of Egypt or the wilderness to the inheritance God offered; the wish was a blasphemy against his goodness, and a fearful one — for God would take them at their word, and those who said "would we had died in this wilderness" should indeed die there.

3:
"And wherefore hath the LORD brought us unto this land, to fall by the sword, that our wives and our children should be a prey? were it not better for us to return into Egypt?" The witnesses note the murmuring rising from the leaders to God himself — charging him with bringing them to ruin. The witnesses note that Gill notes their complaint did not stop at Moses but proceeded against God himself, who had done such wonderful things for them, as if his favours were injuries. The witnesses note that they imagined God had brought them out only to destroy them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the wherefore hath the LORD — the murmuring rising to God himself; the to fall by the sword — accusing him of a design to destroy; the our wives a prey — dreading ruin for their families; the return to Egypt — preferring bondage to trusting him.

The witnesses agree the heart of the sin was a vile suspicion of God: they accused the LORD who had redeemed and fed them of a design to slay them and make their families a prey, and counted it better to return to bondage than trust him; to construe his mercies as malice, and prefer Egypt to Canaan, is the language of an unbelief that has turned the good God into an enemy in its own dark imagining.

4:
"And they said one to another, Let us make a captain, and let us return into Egypt." The witnesses note the people's open rebellion — proposing to choose a captain and go back to Egypt. The witnesses note that Gill notes they would make a head over them instead of Moses, who would never lead them back, and to whom they were now disaffected; Nehemiah says they did appoint one (Neh. 9:17). The witnesses note that this was a formal renouncing of Moses and of God's leading. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the one to another — the people conspiring together; the make a captain — setting up a leader instead of Moses; the return into Egypt — marching back to bondage; the rejecting salvation — undoing the work of their redemption.

The witnesses agree this was rebellion come to a head: not content to murmur, they would cast off Moses, set up a captain of their own, and march back to the house of bondage, undoing the whole work of their redemption; to choose a leader to take them back to Egypt was to reject God's salvation outright — the plainest proof that the heart unweaned from the world will, if it dare, return to its chains rather than follow God into rest.

5:
"Then Moses and Aaron fell on their faces before all the assembly of the congregation of the children of Israel." The witnesses note Moses and Aaron falling on their faces before the whole assembly in grief and intercession. The witnesses note that Gill reads it as done through shame and confusion at so shocking a proposal and such ingratitude, and to supplicate God that he would convince them of their sin and give them repentance. The witnesses agree the prostration was the act of grieved and praying mediators: overwhelmed by the people's blasphemous folly, Moses and Aaron threw themselves on their faces, partly in shame and horror at the sin, partly to entreat both the people to relent and God to spare; the picture of the leaders bowed to the earth while the congregation rages is the very posture of intercession, the faithful pleading where the many rebel.

6:

The witnesses agree the rending of their clothes was a bold and sorrowful witness: alone among the twelve who had searched the land, Joshua and Caleb stood against the whole multitude, tearing their garments in horror at the people's rebellion and unbelief; the faithful minority did not keep silent for fear but openly confronted the lie of the ten and the despair of the many — a courage that risks all to bear true witness for God against a rebellious crowd.

8:

The witnesses agree the two pointed the people to the true ground of victory — not their own strength but God's good pleasure: if the LORD delighted in them, no giant or wall could keep them out; the only thing to fear was their own sin driving him away, and so Wesley marks the condition rightly — the danger lay not in the Canaanites but in provoking God by unbelief and forsaking him.

9:

The witnesses note that Clarke reads "their defence" as tsillam, "their shadow," a figure of protection, the shadow of God's defence over the Canaanites being departed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rebel not — unbelief the only real enemy; the fear not the people — the giants not to be dreaded; the their defence departed — the Canaanites' shadow of protection gone; the the LORD is with us — god himself the shade over his people.

The witnesses agree the two named the only real enemy — not the giants but unbelief: "rebel not" against the LORD, and there is nothing to fear, for the Canaanites' shadow of protection has departed and the LORD is the shade over his own people; the contrast is the heart of faith — the enemy stripped of their defence, and God himself the defence of Israel, so that to fear the people of the land is to forget who fights for them.

10:

11:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, How long will this people provoke me? and how long will it be ere they believe me, for all the signs which I have shewed among them?" The witnesses note God's grieved question — how long would this people provoke him and refuse to believe, despite all his signs? The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD spake out of the cloud upon the tabernacle. The witnesses note that Henry sees here the righteous sentence God gave against Israel for their murmuring and unbelief, showing what their sin deserved. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the how long provoke me — the patience of God tried; the ere they believe me — the sin laid bare as unbelief; the for all the signs — refusing trust against clear evidence; the what most provokes — the settled refusal to credit his word (Heb. 3:19).

The witnesses agree the LORD's complaint lays bare the root of all: the sin was unbelief, and that against the clearest evidence — for after all the signs in Egypt, at the sea, and in the wilderness, they still would not trust him; the question "how long ere they believe me?" shows that what most provokes God is not weakness but the settled refusal to credit his word after he has given every proof of his power and love (cf. Heb. 3:19).

12:

The witnesses note God's threat to destroy the people and make of Moses a greater nation — a proposal to test and move him. The witnesses note that Gill notes that, as to the body of the people, this is not a peremptory decree but spoken partly to draw out Moses' response and partly to bring the people to a sense of their sin. The witnesses note that the offer to make Moses a great nation tried his heart. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the smite with pestilence — the people justly threatened; the disinherit them — their forfeiting of the land deserved; the make of thee a nation — the offer that tested Moses; the to prove his servant — that his love and zeal might shine.

The witnesses agree the threat was real in its justice yet designed to prove Moses: the people deserved to be smitten and disinherited, and God could righteously have done it; but in proposing to make of Moses a greater nation, he laid before his servant the strongest of temptations — to let the rebels perish and rise himself to a patriarch's greatness — that Moses' love for the people and zeal for God's glory might shine the brighter in refusing it.

13:

The reformed reader (Calvin) marks the noble pattern of his prayer: refusing the offered greatness, Moses sets himself in the breach to turn away wrath, and grounds his plea not on the people's worth — they had none — but on God's own honour; his first argument is that the Egyptians would hear of Israel's destruction, and God's name be dishonoured among the heathen — the intercessor pleading God's glory as his strongest reason.

16:
"Because the LORD was not able to bring this people into the land which he sware unto them, therefore he hath slain them in the wilderness." The witnesses note the very slander Moses dreaded — that the nations would say the LORD could not bring Israel in, and so slew them. The witnesses note that Gill spells out the heathen's false conclusion: that God, though he brought them out of Egypt, was not able to bring them through to Canaan, his power having failed. The witnesses note that Wesley puts the slander bluntly — "his power was quite spent in bringing them out of Egypt, and could not finish the work." The witnesses agree the answer is a gentle rebuke wrapped in mercy: the parents had made their children the excuse for unbelief, fearing they would be a prey, yet God would bring those very children safely in, while the fearful parents perished; the land the fathers "despised" the children should "know" — a proof that God's purpose stands sure, and that the unbelief which forfeits the promise for one generation does not cancel it for the next.

32:
"But as for you, your carcases, they shall fall in this wilderness." The witnesses note the sentence pressed home upon the rebels themselves, set against the mercy to their children. The witnesses note that Gill notes the way of speaking distinguishes them from their children. The witnesses note that Wesley marks the contempt with which the rebels are now spoken of — "your carcases" — they having by their sin made themselves vile; Ellicott notes the very words they themselves had used (v.2). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the as for you — set against the mercy to the children; the your carcases — the contemptuous word of judgment; the they shall fall — the personal doom of the rebels; the their own words — "in this wilderness," echoing their murmur (v.2).

The witnesses agree the repetition, set against the promise to the children, drives home the personal doom of the rebels: "as for you" — your carcasses, even yours — shall fall; the contemptuous word marks how low their sin had brought them, and the echo of their own murmur ("in this wilderness," v.2) seals the justice of it, the very phrase they used in unbelief returned upon them as their sentence.

So the witnesses make the verse the rebels' doom pressed: The witnesses note the as for you: set against the mercy to the children. 33:
"And your children shall wander in the wilderness forty years, and bear your whoredoms, until your carcases be wasted in the wilderness." The witnesses note the children made to wander forty years, bearing the consequence of their fathers' unfaithfulness. The witnesses note that Gill reads "wander" as "feed," like shepherds moving from place to place, the children grazing like sheep in the wilderness in search of pasture; the forty years reckoned from the coming out of Egypt. The witnesses note that the children must bear their fathers' "whoredoms," that is, their unfaithfulness to God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the wander forty years — feeding like sheep in the wilderness; the bear your whoredoms — sharing the fruit of the fathers' unfaithfulness; the the innocent burdened — the children feeling the bitterness too; the sin's long consequence — one generation's sin a burden on the next.

The witnesses agree the sentence reached even the innocent in its temporal effect: the children, though they would inherit at last, must first wander the long forty years and feel the bitterness of their fathers' sin, sharing the wilderness life that the parents' unbelief imposed; the lesson is sober — that one generation's unfaithfulness lays a long burden on the next, sin's consequences outlasting the sinners and felt by those who did not commit it.

So the witnesses make the verse the long wandering: The witnesses note the wander forty years: feeding like sheep in the wilderness. The witnesses note the bear your whoredoms: sharing the fruit of the fathers' unfaithfulness. The witnesses note the the innocent burdened: the children feeling the bitterness too. The witnesses note the sin's long consequence: one generation's sin a burden on the next. The expositors observe the wander forty years: feeding like sheep in the wilderness. The expositors observe the bear your whoredoms: sharing the fruit of the fathers' unfaithfulness. The expositors observe the the innocent burdened: the children feeling the bitterness too. 34:
"After the number of the days in which ye searched the land, even forty days, each day for a year, shall ye bear your iniquities, even forty years..." The witnesses note the measure of the sentence — a year for each of the forty days the spies searched the land. The witnesses note that Gill records the reckoning, a day for a year. The witnesses note that Clarke presses the fitness — the spies were forty days searching, and the people who rebelled on their evil report are condemned to forty years' wandering; "now let them make a captain and go back to Egypt if they can," God having so hedged them that they could neither return nor go forward. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the forty days searched — the time of the spies' errand; the each day for a year — the punishment fitted to the sin; the forty years — the measure of their wandering; the hedged in — able neither to return to Egypt nor go forward.

The Wesleyan reader (Clarke) marks the exact justice of the measure: the forty days of the spies' search become forty years of wandering, a year for a day, the punishment fitted precisely to the sin; and he notes the irony of their plight — the people who had proposed a captain to return to Egypt now find themselves hedged in by God's providence, able neither to go back nor to go forward, held fast in the wilderness their unbelief had chosen.

So the witnesses make the verse a year for a day: The witnesses note the forty days searched: the time of the spies' errand. The witnesses note the each day for a year: the punishment fitted to the sin. The witnesses note the forty years: the measure of their wandering. The witnesses note the hedged in: able neither to return to Egypt nor go forward. The expositors observe the forty days searched: the time of the spies' errand. The expositors observe the each day for a year: the punishment fitted to the sin. The expositors observe the forty years: the measure of their wandering. The expositors observe the hedged in: able neither to return to Egypt nor go forward. 35:
"I the LORD have said, I will surely do it unto all this evil congregation, that are gathered together against me: in this wilderness they shall be consumed, and there they shall die." The witnesses note the sentence sealed — "I the LORD have said" — the decree absolute and certain. The witnesses note that Gill notes the decree is absolute and peremptory and will never be revoked, the LORD having determined and resolved it. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read that as surely as Jehovah had spoken it, would he do it to that evil congregation. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the I the LORD have said — the word of God's fixed purpose; the I will surely do it — the decree absolute and certain; the this evil congregation — gathered together against him; the consumed in the wilderness — a settled judgment, irreversible.

The witnesses agree the closing word sets the seal of God's unchangeable purpose on the sentence: where the pardon had spared the nation, this decree on the rebellious generation was fixed and irreversible — "I the LORD have said," and it would surely be done; the solemn certainty teaches that there is a point at which God's patience, long abused, passes into a settled judgment that no further crying can recall, the consumed in the wilderness a warning to all who presume on grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the sealed decree: The witnesses note the I the LORD have said: the word of God's fixed purpose. The witnesses note the I will surely do it: the decree absolute and certain. The witnesses note the this evil congregation: gathered together against him. The witnesses note the consumed in the wilderness: a settled judgment, irreversible. The expositors observe the I the LORD have said: the word of God's fixed purpose. The expositors observe the I will surely do it: the decree absolute and certain. The expositors observe the this evil congregation: gathered together against him. The expositors observe the consumed in the wilderness: a settled judgment, irreversible. The believer is taught to mark the I the LORD have said, the I will surely do it, the this evil congregation, the consumed in the wilderness, and to follow the LORD fully. 36:
"And the men, which Moses sent to search the land, who returned, and made all the congregation to murmur against him, by bringing up a slander upon the land," The witnesses note the ten spies singled out — the men whose slander of the land made all the congregation to murmur. The witnesses note that Gill notes these were the ten, and only they at this time. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the spies who had induced the congregation to revolt by their evil report were now to be made a practical proof of the solemnity of God's threatening. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the men Moses sent — the ten who searched the land; the made all to murmur — stirring the whole congregation; the a slander upon the land — the evil report against God's gift; the the heaviest guilt — those who led others into sin punished first.

The witnesses agree the ringleaders were marked for the first stroke of judgment: the ten who had slandered the good land and stirred up the whole nation to rebel bore the heaviest and swiftest guilt, for they had not only sinned themselves but led others into sin; God would make their death the immediate sign and pledge of the longer sentence on the rest — those who corrupt the many punished first and most.

So the witnesses make the verse the slandering spies: The witnesses note the the men Moses sent: the ten who searched the land. The witnesses note the made all to murmur: stirring the whole congregation. The witnesses note the a slander upon the land: the evil report against God's gift. The witnesses note the the heaviest guilt: those who led others into sin punished first. The expositors observe the the men Moses sent: the ten who searched the land. The expositors observe the made all to murmur: stirring the whole congregation. The expositors observe the a slander upon the land: the evil report against God's gift. The expositors observe the the heaviest guilt: those who led others into sin punished first. 37:
"Even those men that did bring up the evil report upon the land, died by the plague before the LORD." The witnesses note the ten spies struck dead by a plague before the LORD — the first execution of the sentence. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was they, and they only at this time, who died. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read them dying by the plague before the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the evil report — the slander that bred the revolt; the died by the plague — smitten at once before the LORD; the the first stroke — the ringleaders judged immediately; the the earnest of the sentence — a token that God would do as he swore.

The witnesses agree the sudden death of the ten was God's immediate seal upon his word: while the rest of the generation were given forty years to die by degrees, the ringleaders were smitten at once "before the LORD," that all Israel might see the threatening was no idle word but would surely be performed; their fall was the earnest of the whole sentence, a swift token that God would do as he had sworn.

So the witnesses make the verse the spies struck: The witnesses note the the evil report: the slander that bred the revolt. The witnesses note the died by the plague: smitten at once before the LORD. The witnesses note the the first stroke: the ringleaders judged immediately. The witnesses note the the earnest of the sentence: a token that God would do as he swore. The expositors observe the the evil report: the slander that bred the revolt. The expositors observe the died by the plague: smitten at once before the LORD. The expositors observe the the first stroke: the ringleaders judged immediately. The expositors observe the the earnest of the sentence: a token that God would do as he swore. The believer is taught to mark the the evil report, the died by the plague, the the first stroke, the the earnest of the sentence, and to follow the LORD fully. The verse thus records that the ten spies died by the plague before the LORD — the immediate seal on his threatening, the earnest of the whole sentence, that God would surely perform his word.
38:
"But Joshua the son of Nun, and Caleb the son of Jephunneh, which were of the men that went to search the land, lived still." The witnesses note that of the twelve spies, only Joshua and Caleb were preserved alive. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua is here set first, as Caleb is elsewhere, showing they were equal in dignity. The witnesses note that Wesley marks that death never misses his mark nor takes any by oversight who are designed for life, though in the midst of those that are to die. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Joshua and Caleb — the two faithful spies; the of the men that searched — the same company as the ten; the lived still — spared amid the death of the rest; the discriminating providence — death taking none appointed to life.

The witnesses agree the sparing of the two faithful amid the death of the ten is a striking proof of God's discriminating providence: in the very same company, the same errand, the same plague, the unbelieving fell and the faithful "lived still"; Wesley draws the comfort — death takes none by oversight who are appointed to life, God preserving his own in the midst of judgment, the faithful untouched while the guilty are swept away.

So the witnesses make the verse the two preserved: The witnesses note the Joshua and Caleb: the two faithful spies. The witnesses note the of the men that searched: the same company as the ten. The witnesses note the lived still: spared amid the death of the rest. The witnesses note the discriminating providence: death taking none appointed to life. The expositors observe the Joshua and Caleb: the two faithful spies. The expositors observe the of the men that searched: the same company as the ten. The expositors observe the lived still: spared amid the death of the rest. The expositors observe the discriminating providence: death taking none appointed to life. The believer is taught to mark the Joshua and Caleb, the of the men that searched, the lived still, the discriminating providence, and to follow the LORD fully. The verse thus records that Joshua and Caleb alone of the spies lived — God's discriminating providence preserving his faithful in the midst of judgment, death taking none appointed to life.
39:
"And Moses told these sayings unto all the children of Israel: and the people mourned greatly." The witnesses note Moses reporting the sentence, and the people's great mourning — but a sorrow for the punishment, not the sin. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses told them all that murmured, of twenty years and up, should die in the wilderness and never see Canaan. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads their sorrow as for the punishment their sin had entailed, not godly sorrow for the sin itself. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses told them — the sentence reported to all; the mourned greatly — a great but shallow grief; the for the punishment — not godly sorrow for the sin; the worldly sorrow — grieving the consequence, working no repentance (2 Cor. 7:10).

The witnesses agree the people's grief, though great, was of the wrong kind: they mourned the sentence, not the sin — sorry for the loss of Canaan and the doom of the wilderness, but not truly humbled before the God they had provoked; the distinction is searching, for there is a sorrow of the world that grieves only the consequences and works not repentance, as theirs did not, the next day proving their hearts unchanged (cf. 2 Cor. 7:10).

So the witnesses make the verse the wrong sorrow: The witnesses note the Moses told them: the sentence reported to all. The witnesses note the mourned greatly: a great but shallow grief. The witnesses note the for the punishment: not godly sorrow for the sin. The witnesses note the worldly sorrow: grieving the consequence, working no repentance (2 Cor. 7:10). The expositors observe the Moses told them: the sentence reported to all. The expositors observe the mourned greatly: a great but shallow grief. The expositors observe the for the punishment: not godly sorrow for the sin. The expositors observe the worldly sorrow: grieving the consequence, working no repentance (2 Cor. 7:10). The believer is taught to mark the Moses told them, the mourned greatly, the for the punishment, the worldly sorrow, and to follow the LORD fully. The verse thus records that the people mourned the sentence but not the sin — a worldly sorrow grieving only the consequence, working no true repentance (2 Cor. 7:10).
40:
"And they rose up early in the morning, and gat them up into the top of the mountain, saying, Lo, we be here, and will go up unto the place which the LORD hath promised: for we have sinned." The witnesses note the people rising early, now eager to go up to the land they had just refused — confessing "we have sinned." The witnesses note that Gill notes they could not sleep for the bad news, and were now in as great haste to go up and possess Canaan as they had been before to return to Egypt. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read them going up to the top of the mountain, saying, "we be here, and will go up." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rose up early — eager now to go up; the we will go up — to the land just refused; the for we have sinned — confession on the lips; the self-will still — obeying their own will against God's word.

The witnesses agree the sudden reversal was no true repentance but a fresh self-will: the very people who would not go up when God commanded now resolved to go up when he forbade, swinging from despairing unbelief to presumptuous haste; their "we have sinned" was on their lips, but their action proved the heart unchanged — still following their own will against God's word, the same disobedience now wearing the dress of zeal.

So the witnesses make the verse the presumptuous haste: The witnesses note the rose up early: eager now to go up. The witnesses note the we will go up: to the land just refused. The witnesses note the for we have sinned: confession on the lips. The witnesses note the self-will still: obeying their own will against God's word. The expositors observe the rose up early: eager now to go up. The expositors observe the we will go up: to the land just refused. The expositors observe the for we have sinned: confession on the lips. The expositors observe the self-will still: obeying their own will against God's word. The believer is taught to mark the rose up early, the we will go up, the for we have sinned, the self-will still, and to follow the LORD fully. The verse thus records that they rose to go up to the land they had refused — a fresh self-will, not repentance, the same disobedience now wearing the dress of zeal.
41:
"And Moses said, Wherefore now do ye transgress the commandment of the LORD? but it shall not prosper." The witnesses note Moses' warning — that to go up now was as truly a transgression as their refusal had been, and it would not prosper. The witnesses note that Gill notes the command now was to turn back into the wilderness toward the Red sea, which they were transgressing by going forward. The witnesses note that Calvin rejects their feigned penitence, by which sinners "twist round the pot," trying all shifts rather than truly submit to God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the wherefore transgress — going up against the present command; the the commandment of the LORD — now to turn back, not advance; the it shall not prosper — obedience out of season failing; the feigned penitence — self-will twisting to escape on its own terms.

The reformed reader (Calvin) exposes the false repentance: their seeming sorrow was but a shift, an attempt to escape the consequence on their own terms rather than to submit to God; Moses warns that obedience cannot be rendered by doing the right thing at the wrong time against God's command — to go up now, when God said turn back, was a new disobedience, and could not prosper, for self-will dressed as devotion is still rebellion.

So the witnesses make the verse the new transgression: The witnesses note the wherefore transgress: going up against the present command. The witnesses note the the commandment of the LORD: now to turn back, not advance. The witnesses note the it shall not prosper: obedience out of season failing. The witnesses note the feigned penitence: self-will twisting to escape on its own terms. The expositors observe the wherefore transgress: going up against the present command. The expositors observe the the commandment of the LORD: now to turn back, not advance. The expositors observe the it shall not prosper: obedience out of season failing. The expositors observe the feigned penitence: self-will twisting to escape on its own terms. The believer is taught to mark the wherefore transgress, the the commandment of the LORD, the it shall not prosper, the feigned penitence, and to follow the LORD fully. The verse thus records that Moses warned their going up was a new transgression that could not prosper — feigned penitence, self-will dressed as devotion still rebellion against God's word.
42:
"Go not up, for the LORD is not among you; that ye be not smitten before your enemies." The witnesses note Moses' plain warning — go not up, for the LORD is not among them, lest they be smitten. The witnesses note that Gill notes victory is of the Lord, and the Targum adds that the ark, the tabernacle, and the cloud of glory did not move — a plain sign God would not go with them. The witnesses note that without God's presence they could not but be defeated. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD not among you — his presence withdrawn from the venture; the the ark unmoved — the symbols of his presence staying behind; the lest ye be smitten — sure defeat without him; the the one thing needful — presence and victory standing together.

The witnesses agree the whole matter turned on the presence of God: the same people for whom his presence would have made every giant fall were now, without him, sure to fall before the least enemy; the symbols of his presence stayed in the camp, a visible token that he would not go up — and Moses' warning lays bare the one thing needful, that to go to battle without God is to go to certain defeat, presence and victory standing or falling together.

So the witnesses make the verse go not up: The witnesses note the the LORD not among you: his presence withdrawn from the venture. The witnesses note the the ark unmoved: the symbols of his presence staying behind. The witnesses note the lest ye be smitten: sure defeat without him. The witnesses note the the one thing needful: presence and victory standing together. The expositors observe the the LORD not among you: his presence withdrawn from the venture. The expositors observe the the ark unmoved: the symbols of his presence staying behind. The expositors observe the lest ye be smitten: sure defeat without him. The expositors observe the the one thing needful: presence and victory standing together. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD not among you, the the ark unmoved, the lest ye be smitten, the the one thing needful, and to follow the LORD fully. The verse thus records that Moses warned "go not up, for the LORD is not among you" — the one thing needful his presence, and to go without him certain defeat, presence and victory standing or falling together.
43:
"For the Amalekites and the Canaanites are there before you, and ye shall fall by the sword: because ye are turned away from the LORD, therefore the LORD will not be with you." The witnesses note the cause of the coming defeat — they had turned from the LORD, and so he would not be with them against the waiting enemy. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Amalekites and Canaanites were there before them, having removed or detached a party to hold the pass. The witnesses note that the reason given is plain: they had turned from the LORD, and so he would not be with them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the enemy before you — the Amalekites and Canaanites ready; the ye shall fall by the sword — the certain defeat foretold; the turned away from the LORD — the true cause of their ruin; the he will not be with you — left to fall without his help.

The witnesses agree the verse names both the danger and its true cause: the enemy stood ready in the heights, but the real reason of the certain defeat was that Israel had turned from the LORD; the warning fastens not on the strength of the Canaanites but on the absence of God, teaching that the people who forsake him cannot count on his help, and that to turn from God is to be left to fall before the least of foes.

So the witnesses make the verse the cause of defeat: The witnesses note the the enemy before you: the Amalekites and Canaanites ready. The witnesses note the ye shall fall by the sword: the certain defeat foretold. The witnesses note the turned away from the LORD: the true cause of their ruin. The witnesses note the he will not be with you: left to fall without his help. The expositors observe the the enemy before you: the Amalekites and Canaanites ready. The expositors observe the ye shall fall by the sword: the certain defeat foretold. The expositors observe the turned away from the LORD: the true cause of their ruin. The expositors observe the he will not be with you: left to fall without his help. The verse thus records that they should fall by the sword because they had turned from the LORD — the real cause of defeat not the enemy's strength but the absence of the God they had forsaken.
44:
"But they presumed to go up unto the hill top: nevertheless the ark of the covenant of the LORD, and Moses, departed not out of the camp." The witnesses note the people presuming to go up despite the warning, while the ark and Moses stayed in the camp. The witnesses note that Gill notes they went up in a bold, audacious, presumptuous manner, withdrawing from God and his minister, lifted up in themselves and confident of their own strength; the Vulgate reads "being darkened they went up." The witnesses note that the ark of the covenant and Moses did not stir from the camp. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the presumed to go up — venturing against the warning; the lifted up in themselves — trusting their own strength; the the ark departed not — the token of God's presence stayed behind; the nor did Moses — the mediator not going with them.

The witnesses agree the presumption was the crown of their folly: warned that God was not with them, they went up anyway, trusting their own strength, while the ark — the very token of God's presence — and Moses himself stayed behind; the sight of an army marching to battle without God, the ark unmoved in the camp, is the picture of presumption, men venturing in their own confidence what God has forbidden, going up to certain ruin.

So the witnesses make the verse the presumption: The witnesses note the presumed to go up: venturing against the warning. The witnesses note the lifted up in themselves: trusting their own strength. The witnesses note the the ark departed not: the token of God's presence stayed behind. The witnesses note the nor did Moses: the mediator not going with them. The expositors observe the presumed to go up: venturing against the warning. The expositors observe the lifted up in themselves: trusting their own strength. 45:
"Then the Amalekites came down, and the Canaanites which dwelt in that hill, and smote them, and discomfited them, even unto Hormah." The witnesses note the defeat — the Amalekites and Canaanites came down and smote Israel as far as Hormah. The witnesses note that Gill notes the enemies, having the advantage of the higher ground, came down upon the Israelites and smote them, the place named Hormah from this destruction. The witnesses note that the rout reached even to Hormah, "destruction." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the enemies came down — from the advantage of the hill; the smote and discomfited them — israel beaten and chased; the even unto Hormah — a name meaning "destruction."; the presumption's end — the place of pride become the place of ruin.

The witnesses agree the outcome sealed the lesson of the whole chapter: the people who would not go up when God was with them, and went up when he was not, were beaten and chased to "Hormah" — a name meaning destruction; the very victory they presumed on turned to rout, proving that without God all human strength is vain, and that presumption, like unbelief before it, ends in defeat — the place of their pride becoming the place of their ruin.

So the witnesses make the verse the rout at Hormah: The witnesses note the the enemies came down: from the advantage of the hill. The witnesses note the smote and discomfited them: Israel beaten and chased. The witnesses note the even unto Hormah: a name meaning "destruction." The witnesses note the presumption's end: the place of pride become the place of ruin. The expositors observe the the enemies came down: from the advantage of the hill. The expositors observe the smote and discomfited them: Israel beaten and chased. The expositors observe the even unto Hormah: a name meaning "destruction." The expositors observe the presumption's end: the place of pride become the place of ruin.
